graph.confusable_reason: Both attack the lower stem/crown region of soybean, cause wilting and plant death often in patches in the field, and produce light-colored fungal growth near the soil line. Diaporthe (stem canker / pod & stem blight phase) and Southern blight both show stem lesions with wilting, so casual inspection of a dying plant near the soil line can confuse them. The resolving difference lies in the fungal signs (black pycnidia/perithecia vs. white mycelial fan with tan sclerotia).
graph.shared_presentation: Wilting, yellowing, and death of individual soybean plants, frequently in patches, with discolored/lesioned lower stems near the soil line and light-colored fungal growth visible on or around the stem base during warm, humid conditions; both can kill plants prematurely from mid-season onward.

forks.question: What is the color and structure of the fungal growth at the base of the stem and on the soil/debris?
forks.a_signal: Tiny embedded BLACK fruiting bodies (pycnidia in rows for pod & stem blight, or perithecia for stem canker) in the stem tissue; no surface mycelial mat
forks.b_signal: Conspicuous WHITE, coarse, fan-shaped mycelial mat radiating over the stem base and onto soil/debris

forks.question: Are there spherical, mustard-seed-like bodies (sclerotia) on or near the infected stem?
forks.a_signal: Absent — no round tan/brown sclerotia; only flask-shaped black fruiting bodies embedded in tissue
forks.b_signal: Present — numerous spherical sclerotia (~1-3 mm) starting white then maturing to tan/reddish-brown like mustard seeds on the white mat

forks.question: Where on the plant is the lesion/decay concentrated?
forks.a_signal: Cankers can occur on the main stem at nodes (stem canker) or pycnidia scattered on stems and pods (pod & stem blight); lesions often above soil line
forks.b_signal: Decay concentrated at the very base/crown right at the soil line, often girdling the stem with soft rot

forks.question: What is the lesion appearance on the stem?
forks.a_signal: Reddish-brown to dark sunken canker (stem canker) or speckled dark pycnidia in linear rows on dead tissue (pod & stem blight)
forks.b_signal: Light brown, watery, soft rot at the base with shredded/macerated bark, not a discrete sunken canker

forks.question: What soil and temperature conditions accompany the outbreak?
forks.a_signal: Favored by frequent rainfall and extended wet periods; canker phase linked to early-season infection and susceptible varieties across humid regions
forks.b_signal: Favored by hot temperatures (28-35C), high moisture, and presence of surface crop residue/organic mulch at the soil surface

forks.question: How does foliar death proceed?
forks.a_signal: Leaves may yellow/brown but often remain attached (especially stem canker interveinal chlorosis/necrosis); decline over weeks
forks.b_signal: Rapid wilt and death of the whole plant once the base is girdled, leaves often staying attached but plant collapsing quickly

graph.narrative_a: Genus Diaporthe encompasses the Diaporthe/Phomopsis complex on soybean, including Diaporthe stem canker (caused by D. caulivora and D. aspalathi) and Phomopsis/Diaporthe pod and stem blight (D. longicolla and others). Symptoms include reddish-brown sunken cankers at stem nodes, interveinal chlorosis and necrosis of leaves that often remain attached, and—diagnostic for the complex—small black fruiting bodies: linear rows of pycnidia on stems and pods (pod & stem blight) or beaked perithecia in canker tissue (stem canker). The fungus overwinters in residue and seed; disease is favored by prolonged wet weather. Confusion with southern blight arises because both kill plants at or near the stem base, but Diaporthe produces black, tissue-embedded fruiting structures rather than a white surface mycelial mat.
graph.narrative_b: Southern blight, caused by Athelia rolfsii (formerly Sclerotium rolfsii), is a soilborne fungus that attacks the stem base and crown of soybean right at the soil line, especially under hot, humid conditions with abundant surface residue. The hallmark signs are a coarse white fan-shaped mycelial mat growing over the lower stem and adjacent soil/debris, and numerous small spherical sclerotia that begin white and mature to tan or reddish-brown, resembling mustard seeds. The pathogen girdles the stem with a soft, watery rot, causing rapid wilting and collapse of individual plants, often in patches. It is distinguished from Diaporthe by the white mycelium and round sclerotia versus Diaporthe's black, embedded pycnidia/perithecia.
graph.management_a: Plant resistant/tolerant varieties (especially for stem canker), use high-quality pathogen-free seed and seed treatment fungicides for pod & stem blight, rotate away from soybean, manage residue through tillage where appropriate, harvest promptly to limit seed infection, and apply foliar fungicides (e.g., strobilurin/triazole) at R3-R5 in high-risk fields for the pod & stem blight phase.
graph.management_b: Manage through deep burial of crop residue via tillage to reduce surface inoculum, crop rotation with non-host grasses (corn, small grains), avoid excessive surface organic mulch, improve soil drainage and reduce plant stress, and consider in-furrow or banded fungicides in chronically affected fields; sclerotia are long-lived so multi-year rotation is key.

lay.farmer_lay_report: Scattered soybean plants, often in patches, are wilting, turning yellow, and dying earlier than they should from mid-season on. The lower stems right at the soil line look darkened and rotted, and during the warm, humid stretch I can see some light-colored, whitish growth on or around the base of the stems.
lay.decisive_lay_a: No little round tan or brown seed-like balls on the stem; if you look very closely (a hand lens helps) at the dead stem you only see tiny black flask- or pimple-shaped specks buried in the tissue, not loose round balls.
lay.decisive_lay_b: Lots of tiny round balls about the size of mustard seeds (roughly 1-3 mm) sitting on the white mat at the stem base and nearby soil; they start out white and ripen to tan or reddish-brown, looking just like scattered mustard seeds.
